data.title: Johnson & Johnson Expected to Post Higher 4Q Profit, Sales — Earnings Preview
data.description:
By Kelly Cloonan

Johnson & Johnson is set to release fourth-quarter results on Wednesday before the market opens. Here is what you need to know.

PROFIT: The healthcare conglomerate is expected to post a profit of $5.61 billion, or $2.29 a share, according to analysts polled by FactSet. That's compared with $3.43 billion, or $1.41 a share, in the same quarter a year earlier.

ADJUSTED EARNINGS: Analysts expect adjusted earnings of $2.46 a share, according to FactSet.

SALES: Quarterly revenue is projected to rise to $24.1 billion from $22.52 billion a year earlier.

The stock is up 12% in the last three months and was recently trading at $217.89.

WHAT TO WATCH

  • Wall Street will be taking a close look at J&J's guidance for the year ahead. Analysts expect adjusted earnings and sales to rise in 2026 from their projections for 2025, according to FactSet. UBS analysts say they expect further growth in J&J's innovative medicines portfolio, helping offset declines from its Stelara drug, and see signs of resiliency in its medical technology business. "Ultimately, with a plethora of new product launches anticipated across both segments and management's early confidence in 2026, we see estimate revision potential in 2026+ as likely," they say.
  • Analysts will also be looking for any commentary around J&J's longer-term guidance. J&J's management team has appeared increasingly confident it can reach the high end of its target for average annual revenue growth of 5% to 7% from 2025 through 2030, J.P. Morgan analysts say in a note. The company expects double-digit growth later in the decade on accelerated demand for its innovative medicines and medical technology portfolios, the analysts say.
  • J&J's earnings report will come on the heels of its agreement with the Trump administration to lower prices for U.S. customers in exchange for an exemption from tariffs. The agreement, disclosed earlier this month, follows similar drug-pricing deals between the Trump administration and companies like GSK, Merck and Novartis. Raymond James analysts say the deals are "largely irrelevant to company bottom lines and resulting in little to no savings for actual consumers in the near-term."

Write to Kelly Cloonan at kelly.cloonan@wsj.com

data.title: Delaware Supreme Court sides with Moelis over stockholder agreement
data.description:
By Tom Hals

A Moelis & Co shareholder cannot challenge an agreement with Ken Moelis that gives the founder broad control over the investment bank's board, the Delaware Supreme Court ruled on Tuesday.

The ruling is the second in recent weeks by the court that reversed a landmark decision that had sparked outrage among tech founders and other powerful investors, who worried the state's judges were undermining Delaware's business-friendly reputation.

Last month, the court revived Elon Musk's record-breaking compensation from Tesla, worth more than $100 billion.

Tuesday's ruling reverses a 2024 decision by Travis Laster of the Court of Chancery that cast doubt on thousands of stockholder agreements that allow powerful investors like private equity firms and founders to control various aspects of board decisions.

Laster said the Moelis stockholder agreement conflicted with Delaware corporate law, which empowers directors to manage the business to benefit all investors.

The Delaware Supreme Court said Laster erred when he determined that the West Palm Beach Firefighters' Pension Fund was not required to sue within three years of the 2014 Moelis agreement because the arrangement amounted to an ongoing violation of Delaware law. The fund had sued in 2023.

The high court said it did not need to address the validity of the Moelis agreement. In the Musk ruling, the Delaware Supreme Court issued a similar narrow ruling that only addressed the remedy.

Within months of Laster's ruling, Delaware lawmakers rushed to amend the state's widely used corporate law to protect stockholder agreements.

As a result, the ruling by the Delaware Supreme Court will have limited practical impact on the state's corporate law.

data.title: S&P 500, Nasdaq slump to one-month lows as Greenland dispute triggers global selloff
data.description:
Key points:
  • Indexes down: Dow 1.62%, S&P 500 1.80%, Nasdaq 2.03%
  • 3M falls after downbeat annual forecasts
  • Trump may reach Fed chair decision as early as next week, Bessent says
  • Nasdaq slips below 50-day moving average

By David French and Pranav Kashyap

The S&P 500 and Nasdaq Composite dropped to their lowest points in a month on Tuesday, as investors returned from the U.S. long weekend and reacted negatively to fresh tariff threats from President Donald Trump against Europe and renewed volatility in global markets.

The risk-off wave, which also pushed the Dow Jones Industrial Average to its lowest intraday level since January 5, helped vault gold to fresh record highs, knocked stocks lower globally and left U.S. Treasuries wobbling under renewed selling pressure.

Both the S&P 500 and the Nasdaq Composite broke below their 50-day moving averages - an important technical threshold.

Trump said on Saturday additional 10% import tariffs would take effect on February 1 on goods from Denmark, Norway, Sweden, France, Germany, the Netherlands, Finland and Great Britain — all already subject to U.S. tariffs.

THREAT REMINISCENT OF 'LIBERATION DAY'

The tariffs would increase to 25% on June 1 and continue until a deal was reached for the U.S. to purchase Greenland, Trump wrote in a post on Truth Social. Leaders of Greenland, an autonomous territory of Denmark, and Denmark have insisted the island is not for sale.

The reinjection of tariff threats into global markets harkens back to April's "Liberation Day," when Trump's levies on global trade partners pushed the S&P 500 to near bear market territory.

"The way we're looking at it is more of a contained version of what we saw around Liberation Day," said Charlie Ripley, senior investment strategist at Allianz Investment Management.

He added the fact Trump's threat is focused on Europe, instead of globally, explains why Tuesday's moves are not as extensive as they were in April. However, these "environments of uncertainty" create a risk-off tone, which is reflected in equity markets, Ripley said.

The CBOE Volatility Index , also known as Wall Street's fear gauge, touched a two-month high at 20.71 points.

At 1:57 p.m. ET (1857 GMT), the Dow Jones Industrial Average fell 800.63 points, or 1.62%, to 48,558.7, the S&P 500 lost 125.14 points, or 1.8%, to 6,814.87 and the Nasdaq Composite lost 478.33 points, or 2.03%, to 23,037.06.

JAM-PACKED WEEK OF DATA AND EARNINGS

Despite the geopolitical uncertainty around Greenland weighing on U.S. equities sentiment, the U.S. economy remains in a strong position. This resilience, coupled with strong earnings, helped indexes rebound from Liberation Day to trade at record highs.

"It doesn't mean the geopolitical tensions can be ignored, but nonetheless, we're coming from a good spot economically on the U.S. side," said Ripley.

Investors are due a host of fresh data this week on the state of the U.S. economy, including the third-quarter U.S. GDP update, January PMI readings and the Personal Consumption Expenditures report, which is the Federal Reserve's preferred inflation gauge.

Earnings season is also kicking into higher gear. Several industry bellwethers, including Intel and Netflix , are set to report their quarterly earnings this week.

Netflix gained 0.3%, after switching to an all-cash offer for Warner Bros Discovery's studio and streaming assets without increasing its $82.7 billion bid.

The streaming company, due to report its quarterly earnings after the bell, was the only stock in the mega-cap "FAANG" group of tech stocks - Meta, Apple, Amazon, Netflix, and Google - to trade higher.

Industrial bellwether 3M tumbled 8.1% after forecasting annual adjusted profit a touch below Wall Street expectations, while Fastenal fell 2.9% after missing fourth-quarter revenue estimates.

Of the 33 S&P 500 companies that had reported as of Friday, 84.8% topped analysts' expectations, according to data compiled by LSEG.

Trump could decide on the next Federal Reserve chair as soon as next week, Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent told CNBC - keeping markets uneasy after the administration recently threatened to indict Chair Jerome Powell.

Markets are also watching speeches by global leaders at the World Economic Forum in Davos, Switzerland.

Among other stocks, RAPT Therapeutics soared 63.8% after Britain's GSK agreed to buy the U.S. firm for $2.2 billion.

data.title: AeroVironment Falls on Stop Work Order for Space Force Contract
data.description:
By Elias Schisgall

Shares of AeroVironment slid after the company said it had received a stop work order on its contract option to provide two BADGER phased array antenna systems for the U.S. Space Force.

The stock dropped 15%, to $333.00, Tuesday afternoon, but has nearly doubled in value in the past year.

The defense contractor said Tuesday that it and the U.S government had mutually agreed to the stop work order, which would halt the delivery of the BADGER systems for the Satellite Communication Augmentation Resource program.

It said the order allows the company to negotiate with the government for an amended contract under new requirements for the SCAR program. That amendment, like the initial contract, is expected to be a firm-fixed-price contract.

"The Company expects to continue to deliver capabilities and products for the SCAR program," AeroVironment said.

Write to Elias Schisgall at elias.schisgall@wsj.com

data.title: Trump's Set to Roll Out Affordability Plans in Davos. What to Expect. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Joe Light

President Donald Trump is trying to convince Americans he has a strategy to lower costs, a top issue for voters as the November midterm elections inch closer. His geopolitical ambitions are throwing a wrench into those plans.

Trump intends to give a speech at 8:30 a.m. Eastern time on Wednesday at the World Economic Forum in Davos-Klosters, Switzerland. The White House has said the address will lay out Trump's affordability agenda, including a suite of policies to lower housing costs.

The stakes are high not just for Trump, but for GOP lawmakers in the House of Representatives and Senate — who are finding their election prospects dragged down by voters' perceptions that the president hasn't focused enough on prices.

About 42% of Americans say the economy and cost of living is the most impost important issue facing the country today, a CNN poll this month found. On that issue, Trump is severely underwater, with 61% of adults in the poll saying they disapprove of his performance versus 39% who approve, the worst marks Trump has registered in either of his two terms.

High inflation helped kill the election bids of former President Joe Biden and Vice President Kamala Harris — and if history is any guide, Trump may struggle to turn his own numbers around.

"Once presidential approval ratings dip, it's fairly difficult to bring them back north," said Beacon Policy Advisors analyst Christopher Niebuhr. "It takes a lot to move economic sentiment."

White House officials have said Trump during the Davos speech will unveil a litany of strategies to bring down housing costs.

The president's already announced that he's asking Congress to ban purchases of single-family homes by large institutional investors such as Blackstone, a long-stated goal of progressives and other populists who say investors squeeze out other home buyers. He's also directed loan giants Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac to buy $200 billion-worth of mortgage-backed securities, a move that some analysts say could lower the rate on a 30-year mortgage by 0.25 percentage point by the end of 2026.

Last week, National Economic Council Director Kevin Hassett said the Trump administration wanted to allow Americans to take money out of their 401(k)s to use for a down payment, which may require Congress to pass legislation first. At various times, Trump and White House officials have also floated the idea of creating new tax-advantaged accounts to save for down payments and to eliminate capital-gains taxes on home sales.

Other ideas that could be on the table include pricing cuts at government mortgage agencies, pressuring home builders to speed up construction, and pressuring title insurers and credit bureaus to lower borrowers' closing costs, wrote TD Cowen analyst Jaret Seiberg in a research note on Tuesday.

Beyond housing, earlier this month Trump called for credit-card interest rates to be capped at 10% for one year. Perhaps most important, Trump sees lowering interest rates as the number-one criterion for his Federal Reserve chair nominee, whose nomination could be announced as soon as this month.

It's far from certain that the blitz of proposals will lower prices, let alone turn around voter perceptions.

The housing proposals announced so far "won't materially improve homeownership. The proposals largely aim to support housing demand, but given the housing supply shortage, they will only lead to firmer house prices, not better affordability," said Moody's Analytics Chief Economist Mark Zandi.

Even a ban on investor home purchases, which would should lower buyer competition, won't help with affordability since it would also lift rents and make it more difficult for renters to save for a down payment, Zandi said.

Another reason to doubt the success of much of Trump's affordability agenda is its reliance on Congress's ability to pass legislation. Many GOP lawmakers have already flatly rejected a legislative cap on credit-card rates, arguing that banks would simply stop offering credit to higher risk borrowers. Changing the tax code to allow for new down payment savings accounts, penalty-free 401(k) withdrawals, and eliminating home-sale capital gains would also be a tall order, especially as lawmakers spend less time in Washington as the elections approach.

But the biggest problem with the White House's affordability push might be Trump's wider geopolitical goals — and his own doubt that high costs are even an issue worth addressing. At a Detroit speech last week, Trump called affordability "a fake word by Democrats" even as he outlined his plans to improve it.

Trump's Detroit speech "was clearly designed to show a focus on domestic issues but given the world is in absolute chaos, it seems hard for him to give it the focus Americans are expecting," said Republican political strategist Jason Cabel Roe. Roe said the GOP's election prospects could still improve if economic growth accelerates during the year.

Trump also has shown no sign that he plans to let up on the use of tariffs as a geopolitical tool. Trump over the weekend said he would impose 10% tariffs on eight European countries as soon as Feb. 1 and would increase them to 25% in June until the U.S. reaches an agreement to buy Greenland, a move that could raise prices on imports. On Monday, he suggested the U.S. could impose 200% tariffs on French wine if the country refuses to join his "Board of Peace."

The day after Trump announced that Fannie and Freddie would buy $200 billion of mortgage bonds, the 30-year fixed-rate mortgage plummeted to 6.06% from 6.21%, according to Mortgage News Daily data. Following the remarks about tariffs on eight European nations, the rate soared 0.14 percentage point to 6.21% as of midday Tuesday.

Write to Joe Light at joe.light@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Dow Sinks 800 Points, Treasury Prices Drop — WSJ
data.description:
Stocks tumbled Tuesday as markets moved back in trade-war mode.

The Dow industrials lost about 800 points, more than 1.5%, the dollar weakened and Treasury yields rose, touching their highest levels since September. Investors sought safety in traditional havens, pushing gold prices to a fresh record. The Swiss franc rallied.

President Trump doubled down on his desire to take over Greenland, firing off social-media posts on his way to the World Economic Forum in Switzerland. He is expected to spar with leaders there about his territorial ambitions and his threats to torpedo the U.S.-European trade relationship if he doesn't get what he wants. His latest warning shot: possible 200% tariffs on French Champagne.

Among U.S. benchmarks, the Nasdaq composite dropped 2% and the S&P 500 lost 1.8%. Big technology names such as Nvidia, Alphabet and Amazon skidded, with all of the so-called Magnificent Seven growth stocks down at least 1.4%.

Elsewhere, Japan's long-term government bond yields leapt to record highs, on concern that a coming election could lead to a tax cut that dents the country's finances. Global bond yields rose.

The dollar retreated against the yen, euro and Swiss franc. Silver futures rallied to a new record above $94 a troy ounce and gold topped $4,750 a troy ounce with its own record.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: JinkoSolar Shares Fall After U.S. Rejects Challenge to First Solar Patent
data.description:
By Amira McKee

Shares of JinkoSolar fell after the United States Patent and Trademark office declined to invalidate competitor First Solar's patents.

The shares fell 12.8% to $25.78 in Tuesday afternoon trading. The stock has risen 21% in the past year.

JinkoSolar, Canadian Solar and Mundra Solar filed applications to invalidate two of First Solar's U.S. patents covering methods of manufacturing TOPCon crystalline silicon photovoltaic solar cells.

JinkoSolar and Canadian Solar filed against First Solar's U.S. patent No. 9,130,074, while Mundra Solar's application sought a review of First Solar's U.S. patent No. 9,666,732.

The inter partes reviews were filed in 2025, and declined on Nov. 20, Dec. 11, and Dec. 18, respectively.

First Solar has pending lawsuits against the three companies alleging infringements of its patents in the United States District Court for the District of Delaware.

Write to Amira McKee at amira.mckee@wsj.com

data.title: Constellation Software's Stock Seen Hit by Overdone AI Fears — Market Talk
data.description: Constellation Software's current valuation reflects assumptions that are far more pessimistic than the company's fundamentals justify, according to CIBC's Stephanie Price. In a report, the analyst says the stock, which is down 18% year-to-date, is priced with "a material reduction in organic growth, and/or flat to negative terminal growth." She says that this is an unlikely scenario, and that Constellation will more likely continue to spend on M&A at a healthy pace, supported by strong cash generation and an attractive environment for buying software businesses. Price says that the stock is pressured as the market assesses AI risks to Constellation's model, even though the company has shown no signs of slowing its dealmaking. (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

data.title: Argentina Trade Surplus Expands in December
data.description:
Argentina’s trade surplus rose to USD 1,892 million in December 2025, up from USD 1,682 million a year earlier.

Imports rose 3.5% year-on-year to USD 5,556 million, driven by a 3.0% increase in quantities and a 0.4% rise in prices.

Meanwhile, exports rose 5.7% to USD 7,448 million, led by a 6.2% increase in quantities despite a 0.5% decline in prices.

For the year-to-date through December 2025, the trade surplus stood at USD 11,286 million, down from USD 18,928 million in the same period of 2024.

data.title: Berkshire Stock Is Getting Cheaper. When Will the Company Resume Buybacks? — Barrons.com
data.description:
Andrew Bary

One of the important issues on the plate of Berkshire Hathaway's new CEO, Greg Abel, is whether to buy back stock — both for the company and for himself.

Berkshire hasn't bought back a share since May 2024.

The stock looks tempting now because it has badly lagged the S&P 500 index since the Berkshire annual meeting in May 2025, and the stock's valuation is lower now by one important measure than it was in May 2024.

Berkshire's class A stock, which is off 2.2% Tuesday at $724,200, is down 10% from its peak of about $809,000 just before the annual meeting — when CEO Warren Buffett surprised shareholders by saying he would step down at year-end while remaining chairman. The S&P 500 is up about 20% since then.

Berkshire now is valued at 1.45 times what Barron's estimates was year-end 2025 book value of about $500,000 per class A share. Book ended the third quarter at around $485,000 per class A share.

When Berkshire last repurchased stock in May 2024, it paid close to $627,000 per A share, which was 1.5 times the June 30, 2024 book value.

Berkshire stock likely would get a lift from a resumption of meaningful stock buyback activity. It would signal the company finally views its stock as sufficiently cheap to repurchase and that Abel views buybacks as a good use of capital. Berkshire will disclose any stock buybacks for the fourth quarter in its earnings release expected around the end of February.

Berkshire's buyback authority had been lodged with Buffett, and it remains to be seen how much leeway Abel has to make that decision on his own, or whether Buffett and the Berkshire board will be involved in that capital allocation decision. Buffett has said Abel will be in charge of capital allocation as CEO.

Looking out to the end of 2026, book value could top $540,000 per class A share, Barron's estimates, meaning the stock trades for less than 1.4 times that projection. Any buyback today could look good in hindsight as book value grows. Berkshire stock has traded between 1.3 and 1.8 times book value in recent years, peaking just before last year's annual meeting.

Buffett has said that book value is a less relevant measure that fails to capture Berkshire's growing earnings power. He prefers intrinsic value — but he doesn't disclose his estimate of it. Book value, despite its limitations, remains a good valuation benchmark for the company.

Berkshire stock trades for about 22 times estimated 2026 earnings — in line with the S&P 500. But the Berkshire "look-through" multiple that gives the company credit for the profits of companies in its $300 billion equity portfolio is estimated to be under 20 based on estimated 2026 earnings.

Berkshire has the wherewithal of a huge stock buyback given that it's sitting on o ver $350 billion of cash. The company recently closed on a nearly $10 billion deal to buy Occidental Petroleum's chemical business but that amounts to less than a quarter's free cash flow. Berkshire hasn't been doing much with its cash, with the company making few acquisitions in recent years and being a net seller of stocks.

Another issue for Abel is whether to purchase stock personally. His most recent purchases came in September 2022 and March 2023 with the latter buy of 55 A shares occurring at about $447,000 per class A share, or about 1.3 times the company's then book value.

Since Berkshire doesn't give stock compensation to its executives — or any employees — management and the board need to buy shares in the open market.

Abel owns 228 A shares — plus a small amount of B shares — worth a total of about $167 million, based on the 2025 Berkshire proxy. That's a sizable amount, but he's wealthy and likely can afford to purchase more. His biggest score was the sale of a 1% interest in Berkshire Hathaway Energy back to the company in 2022 for $870 million in cash. He has also been amply compensated, earning about $20 million annually in recent years and he is slated to get a $25 million cash salary in 202 6, Berkshire recently disclosed.

Buffett sets the standard for wealth concentration. He has over 99% of his wealth in Berkshire stock — his stake is now worth over $140 billion. Abel likely has under half of his net worth in Berkshire stock.

An open-market purchase of Berkshire stock by Abel, 63, would signal that he views the stock attractively and that he's further aligning himself with Berkshire holders.

Berkshire directors might consider following Abel's lead if he buys stock.

There have been no purchases by current directors other than Abel since 2020, when investment manager and Barron's Roundtable member Meryl Witmer bought stock near Berkshire's Covid low at around $260,000 per A share.

Many of the dozen or so directors do own sizable stakes. The board member with the smallest holding is Susan Decker with about $1.5 million of stock. She has been a board member since 2007. She is a former Yahoo executive and is the CEO of Raftr, a community experience platform, according to the Berkshire 2025 proxy.

Write to Andrew Bary at andrew.bary@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: US judge says South Dakota can't push nonprofit to take down abortion pill ads, for now
data.description:
By Daniel Wiessner

A federal judge has temporarily blocked the South Dakota Attorney General's office from seeking to stop a nonprofit, that helps women access abortion pills, from advertising its services in the state, which has a near-total abortion ban.

U.S. District Judge Katherine Polk Failla in Manhattan in a brief order on Saturday barred Attorney General Marty Jackley, a Republican, from taking "any coercive action, formal or informal" to push Mayday Health to remove information on abortion drugs from its website and cease advertising at gas stations around the state.

Mayday Health sued Jackley earlier this month after his office filed a petition in South Dakota state court to bar the ads, which say "Pregnant? Don't want to be?" and include the address of Mayday's website.

Jackley's office says the ads violate a state law banning deceptive trade practices. But Mayday, which does not prescribe or distribute abortion pills, claims the state's actions violate its free-speech rights under the U.S. Constitution.

Failla did not explain her decision on Saturday. She said she would hold a hearing no later than Jan. 30 to decide whether to extend her ruling pending the outcome of the lawsuit.

Jackley's office and Mayday did not immediately respond to requests for comment on Tuesday.

The case is the latest legal battle over access to the drug mifepristone, which is used in 60% of U.S. abortions and has become a focal point in a nationwide litigation over abortion since the U.S. Supreme Court eliminated the constitutional right to the procedure in 2022.

Many Republican-led states have banned or significantly restricted abortion and are now seeking to curb access to mifepristone, which can be prescribed remotely and distributed through the mail.

Six states in three separate cases are challenging federal regulations that made it easier to access mifepristone or approved different versions of the drug, including the initial approval by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration in 2000.

The case is Mayday Health v. Jackley, U.S. District Court for the Southern District of New York, No. 1:26-cv-00078.

For Mayday: Adam Sieff and others from Davis Wright Tremaine

For South Dakota: Amanda Miller of the South Dakota Attorney General's office

Read more:

US abortion pill access under fire: Lawsuits and regulatory battles to watch in 2026

U.S. Supreme Court overturns Roe v. Wade, ends constitutional right to abortion

Texas, Florida mount latest legal challenge to FDA abortion pill approval

data.title: Spain Issues 15 Billion Euros in New April 2036 Government Bond — Update
data.description:
By Emese Bartha

Spain issued 15 billion euros ($17.47 billion) in a new 10-year, April 2036-dated government bond via a bank syndicate on Tuesday, one of the lead manager banks said.

The spread on the bond was set at 6 basis points above the mid-year of the October 2035-dated Spanish bond.

The new bond has a 3.30% coupon, and it was priced at 99.813, at a yield of 3.323%, the same bank said.

Barclays, BBVA, J.P. Morgan, Morgan Stanley, Santander and Societe Generale were lead managers of the deal.

Write to Emese Bartha at emese.bartha@wsj.com

data.title: Exicure Hitron Sells Over 700K Shares
data.description:

data.title: Inspira Global to buy stake in India's Burger King operator RBA for about $50.5 million
data.description:
Restaurant Brands Asia said on Tuesday that Inspira Global will acquire a controlling stake in the Burger King India and Indonesia operator for around 4.60 billion rupees ($50.54 million).

Inspira will buy the entire shareholding of 11.26% in Restaurant Brands Asia (RBA) from QSR Asia, the investment vehicle owned by Everstone.

In addition, Inspira plans to invest about 900 crore rupees through a preferential allotment of shares and another 600 crore rupees through warrants, RBA said in a statement.

These actions will trigger an open offer to RBA's public shareholders, it added.

RBA has a market capitalisation of $408.4 million in Mumbai, according to data compiled by LSEG.

Reuters reported on Monday that RBA was set to bring in a new strategic investor, with the family office of the founders of India's Ajanta Pharma expected to participate, potentially injecting up to 8 billion Indian rupees ($88 million) in the company.

($1 = 91.0260 Indian rupees)

data.title: Gold Skyrockets As Trump Doubles Down On Greenland
data.description:
WASHINGTON (dpa-AFX) - Gold prices catapulted to unprecedented levels on Tuesday as U.S. President Donald Trump's continued push to take over Greenland drove safe-haven demand.

Front Month Comex Gold for January delivery zoomed by $171.20 (or 3.73%) to $4,759.60 per troy ounce. Notably, this is a new record high for gold prices.

Front Month Comex Silver for January delivery also skyrocketed by $6.1150 (or 6.94%) to $94.206 per troy ounce. Notably, this is also a new record high for silver prices.

Despite opposition from European nations and criticism from Russia and China, citing future Russian threats, Trump stepped up efforts to bring the Denmark-owned territory of Greenland in the Arctic under U.S. control.

Committed to push the issue at the World Economic Forum at Davos in Switzerland, Trump also shared an image depicting Greenland as a part of 'U.S. territory.' Further, in a supposedly AI-generated map shared by Trump, Canada and Venezuela are shown as U.S. territories.

Reportedly, U.S.-Canada North American Aerospace Defense Command (NORAD) aircraft is heading to Greenland to land at Pituffik Space Base.

The main session of the WEF began on Monday. The annual meeting is set to wrap up on January 23. Trump is expected to deliver a keynote address on Wednesday.

Earlier, Trump threatened to impose 10% tariffs from February 1 (with the chances of increasing to 25% by June) on eight European countries that refuse to align with the U.S. on the Greenland issue.

European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen warned that Trump's remarks are risking pushing U.S.-EU relations into a 'downward spiral.'

At the same time, she assured that the EU will deliver an 'unflinching' response to his threats. The EU chief announced that the EU bloc is readying a package to consolidate the Arctic security.

Earlier this month, Trump successfully directed U.S. forces to capture the president of Venezuela Nicolas Maduro and now has 'virtually' taken control of its oil industry.

Experts are concerned that Trump may adventure something similar in Greenland.

In the ongoing Russia-Ukraine war, Russia launched ballistic missiles targeting Ukraine's capital Kyiv and a barrage of drone attacks on key energy infrastructure installations.

Ukraine's President Volodymyr Zelenskyy stated that he is expecting to sign documents with the U.S. on post-war security guarantees on the U.S. peace proposal to end the war, while Ukraine's top military commander announced that Russia is not indicating any visible sign of interest in peace talks.

In the Middle East, a U.S.-based Human Rights agency reported that over 4,000 have been killed in the recent protests that began on December 28 against the clerical regime in Iran.

Earlier, Trump warned Iran of military strikes if the government unleashed violence against demonstrators but later retreated, stating that he had confirmed reports that the 'killings have stopped.'

Reports are indicating that the U.S. is consolidating forces at Diego Garcia military base in the Indian Ocean, keeping U.S.-Iran conflict tensions alive.

In the U.S., data released by the Automatic Data Processing today revealed that private employers added an average of 8,000 jobs per week for the four weeks ending December 27, down from an average gain of 11,250 in the previous period.

The U.S. dollar index was last seen trading at 98.49, down by (0.91%) today.

data.title: Intuit on Track for Lowest Close Since November 2023 — Data Talk
data.description:
Intuit Inc. (INTU) is currently at $529.96, down $15.32 or 2.81%

  • Would be lowest close since Nov. 10, 2023, when it closed at $528.89
  • On pace for largest percent decrease since Jan. 14, 2026, when it fell 6.39%
  • Currently down seven consecutive days; down 18.81% over this period
  • Longest losing streak since Sept. 6, 2022, when it fell for seven straight trading days
  • Worst seven-day stretch since the seven days ending March 20, 2020, when it fell 22.7%
  • Down 20% month-to-date
  • Down 20% year-to-date
  • Down 34.36% from its all-time closing high of $807.39 on July 30, 2025
  • Down 13.04% from 52 weeks ago (Jan. 21, 2025), when it closed at $609.41
  • Down 34.36% from its 52-week closing high of $807.39 on July 30, 2025
  • Would be a new 52-week closing low
  • Traded as low as $529.79; lowest intraday level since Nov. 13, 2023, when it hit $523.32
  • Down 2.84% at today's intraday low

All data as of 1:53:52 PM ET

Source: Dow Jones Market Data, FactSet

data.title: Guardian Roofing Partners with Owens Corning & Habitat for Humanity on Roof Deployment Project-- New Roof for Tacoma Veteran Home
data.description:
TACOMA, WA / ACCESS Newswire / January 20, 2026 / Guardian Roofing, Gutters & Insulation, a leading roofing, gutters, and insulation contractor in the Pacific Northwest, is proud to announce a new partnership with Owens Corning and Habitat for Humanity as part of the national Roof Deployment Project. Through this collaboration, Guardian Roofing installed a complete new roof this October for Nancy, the deserving owner of a home located in Tacoma, WA-- at no cost to her.

The Roof Deployment Project is a long-standing initiative by Owens Corning and Habitat for Humanity designed to honor and support U.S. military veterans and their families. Eligible homeowners receive new roofs donated by Owens Corning, installed by certified contractors volunteering their labor through the program. Since its inception in 2016, this national effort has delivered hundreds of safe, durable roofs for honorably discharged veterans and their families across the country.

"We are honored to partner with Owens Corning and Habitat for Humanity 2on this meaningful project," said Mat Rzucidlo, President of Guardian Roofing, Gutters & Insulation. "Building a roof may be our trade - but helping a veteran feel safe, secure, and proud in their home is something far more important. This project embodies what we stand for: quality, community, respect, and giving back."

About the Project

  • Roof materials - Donated by Owens Corning, ensuring top-quality shingles, underlayment, and roofing systems recognized for durability and performance.

  • Installation - Performed by Guardian Roofing's certified crews as part of the Roof Deployment Project volunteer effort.

  • Eligibility - The program supports honorably discharged veterans or their surviving spouses whose existing roof is in need of replacement and whose household meets income eligibility guidelines. Habitat for Humanity affiliates coordinate applications, screening, and eligibility verification.

  • Cost to homeowner - None. Owens Corning supplies materials, and Guardian Roofing provides labor.

For Nancy, the new roof will not only restore safety and comfort to her home but also provides peace of mind - a secure shelter for her family and a tribute to her service.

For Guardian Roofing, this project reinforces the company's commitment to community, veteran support, and high craftsmanship. "We believe every veteran deserves a roof over their head that's strong, safe, and built to last," said Rzucidlo.

For the broader Tacoma and Pacific Northwest community, this partnership demonstrates how thoughtful collaboration among industry leaders - manufacturer, nonprofit, and contractor - can deliver real impact and strengthen neighborhoods.

About Guardian Roofing, Gutters & Insulation

Founded in 2005, Guardian Roofing, LLC is an award-winning professional roofing contractor in the Portland and Seattle markets serving Washington state counties of King, Pierce, Snohomish, Kitsap, Clark, and Thurston; and Oregon counties of Marion, Washington, Multnomah, Clackamas and Yamhill, as well.

Founders Lori and Matt Swanson have more than 60 years of combined roofing experience and employ a team of more than 100 skilled and professionally certified craftsmen who assist customers with their roof, gutter, attic, masonry, and skylight needs. In 2024, Guardian Roofing, Gutters & Insulation, an A+-rated company by the Better Business Bureau, was named as a top 100 Roofing Contractor in the U.S. by Roofing Contractors Magazine. The family-owned company was also recognized in 2022 as one of the fastest-growing private companies by the Puget Sound Business Journal. For more information, visit www.GuardianHome.com. Licenses: WA UBI 604-059-205 | FEIN #81-4469822

data.title: Elektros Inc. Outlines Strategic Objectives Moving Forward
data.description:
SUNNY ISLES BEACH, FL / ACCESS Newswire / January 20, 2026 / For those who may not have seen our prior announcement, Elektros Inc. is pleased to reaffirm and outline its strategic objectives moving forward. The Company remains focused on advancing its lithium operations in Sierra Leone to meet the accelerating global demand for critical battery materials essential to electric vehicles and energy storage solutions.

As noted by Benzinga, the strategic importance of lithium continues to grow globally: "As the backbone of modern batteries powering everything from smartphones to electric vehicles, lithium's importance to the world economy cannot be overstated." - Benzinga

Elektros Inc. (OTC PINK:ELEK) is strategically focused on the advancement and development of its hard‑rock lithium project in Sierra Leone, Africa.

The Company has executed a joint venture ground lease agreement for mineral rights within the Tinkoko Chiefdom of Bo District, maintaining a 75% controlling interest while ensuring full compliance with Sierra Leonean mining regulations.

As of September 2025, Elektros Inc. has obtained an artisanal mining license and has stockpiled approximately 54 metric tons of hard‑rock lithium ore, positioned for export to the United States upon securing shipping capital.

Near‑term objectives include establishing continuous extraction and export operations, executing regular container shipments, securing long‑term offtake agreements with U.S. lithium refineries, and attracting strategic investment partners.

Cautionary Language Concerning Forward‑Looking Statements

This release contains forward‑looking statements that involve risks and uncertainties. Actual results may differ materially. For additional information, please refer to the Company's filings with the SEC at www.sec.gov. Elektros Inc. undertakes no obligation to update forward‑looking statements.

Contact:

Elektros Inc.

Website: www.elektros.energy

SOURCE: Elektros, Inc.
